title: Cache hierarchy and the cost of a miss
description: L1/L2/L3 and DRAM differ in latency by orders of magnitude, so data layout and reuse, not op count, usually decide hot-path speed.
body: Use when reasoning about why a loop is slow despite few instructions. Each level up the hierarchy is dramatically slower — an L1 hit is a few cycles, an LLC miss to DRAM is hundreds — so a cache miss can cost more than dozens of arithmetic ops. The hardware prefetcher hides latency only for predictable (usually unit-stride) access. Do not count operations and ignore the memory access pattern. Actions: estimate working-set size against cache levels, favor sequential reuse, and measure miss rates with PMU counters. Validation signals include miss-rate counters that explain the runtime. Risk boundary: optimizing instruction count while leaving a random access pattern wastes effort; let the counters, not intuition, pick the target.
